g.data-narration: Welcome to Android Activity Lifecycle - the foundation of Android UI development.
An Activity represents a single screen with UI. It's the fundamental building block of Android apps.
g.text: The Android system manages Activities through a stack - new activities move to top.

g.data-narration: Proper lifecycle management saves user data, prevents crashes, and manages resources like camera and GPS.
The six core lifecycle states: Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, and Destroyed.
onCreate initializes the activity and UI. onStart makes it visible to the user.
onResume makes the activity interactive - it's at the top of the activity stack and ready for user input.
g.text: Activity now interactive & at top of stack

g.data-narration: onPause is called when activity is partially obscured. Save data here as it's always called before stopping.
onStop is called when activity is no longer visible. Release heavy resources here - system may kill the process.
onRestart is special - it's called only when returning from stopped state, before onStart.
g.text: Called before onStart when returning from stopped

g.data-narration: onDestroy is the final callback - perform all cleanup here to prevent memory leaks.
This visual flow shows how activities transition between states with their callback methods.
Use onSaveInstanceState to save data and restore it in onCreate for configuration changes.
Three key scenarios: starting new activity, returning to previous, and configuration changes like rotation.
Remember: keep onCreate lightweight, save critical data in onPause, and release resources early.
g.text: Master the lifecycle = Build robust Android apps!
